comment_content:
My Honest Review of Bridgerton Season 3: Disappointing and Unconvincing

As someone who truly enjoyed the first two seasons of Bridgerton, Season 3 was a major letdown. What once felt like a charming, romantic escape has now taken a sharp turn into something that feels forced, overly political, and disconnected from the emotional depth that made the series special.

One of the biggest issues for me was how the show handled inclusivity. Rather than letting diversity evolve naturally within the story, it felt like characters and plotlines were changed just to tick boxes or follow a current agenda. The changes didnΓÇÖt feel earned ΓÇö they felt like they were there for optics or to push a message, not because they genuinely served the narrative or characters.

FrancescaΓÇÖs storyline is a perfect example. Her quiet, beautiful love story with John Stirling was one of the most heartfelt parts of the season. But right after their wedding, weΓÇÖre suddenly introduced to Michaela (a gender-swapped version of Michael from the books), and Francesca is instantly shown to be drawn to her. It completely undermines her relationship with John and makes it feel like her love for him was never real. That twist felt unnatural, rushed, and emotionally dishonest ΓÇö as if everything we just watched didnΓÇÖt matter.

Then thereΓÇÖs Benedict. Early in the season, there are strong hints that he might be exploring his sexuality, only for him to abruptly return to being the typical ΓÇ£manly, straight guyΓÇ¥ who falls for a woman obsessed with chandeliers. That entire subplot felt shallow and inconsistent. If the writers were going to explore BenedictΓÇÖs bisexuality, they should have committed to it with depth and care ΓÇö not thrown in vague suggestions and then dropped it.

Overall, the tone of the season was off ΓÇö much more negative and even a bit depressing at times. The lighthearted, romantic, ΓÇ£feel-goodΓÇ¥ vibe that defined Bridgerton was missing. Instead, it felt like the show was trying too hard to be politically relevant, at the cost of genuine storytelling and character development.

Unfortunately, this season has made me lose interest in the show. I probably wonΓÇÖt be watching future seasons.
reply_content:
I do agree with you that Michaela's introduction undermined Francesca's love story with John. I remember Benedict telling John that his mother is obsessed with great love stories, and that's why Violet wasn't enthusiastic about their story. Later on, she realizes that love isn't always so passionate and epic. It can burn slowly. That was the overall message of John and Francesca's love story. When Michaela appeared, I was very disappointed with the plot twist. I'm going to be honest, I never realized that Francesca was drawn to Michaela until I saw the comments.

On the other hand, I disagree with you on your statement regarding Benedict's sexuality. I think the writers never intended to explore his sexuality to begin with. That subplot was meant to show Benedict's character development. He is now brave enough to live outside society's expectations. Which is where Sophie comes in. Do you remember during season 1 when he confronted Mr. Grandville about his affair with Mr. Wetherby? Mr. Grandville explained to him what it's like to be with someone and feel like you are oceans apart. Loving someone without being able to touch them or ever smile at them is torture. Also, Sophie isn't "obsessed" with the chandelier. If you read the book, you would understand why she was fascinated by its beauty. In this scene, Benedict was drawn to her because she wasn't like the rest of the people of the ton. She was in her own bubble and happy with where she was. Part of Benedict's personality is that he appreciates art. The masquerade ball was very gorgeous, but no one actually paid attention; she was the only one who stopped and took in all that beauty.
